title: Cottage pie
subtitle: Minced beef in a rich gravy, topped with buttered mashed potato
excerpt: Cottage pie is minced beef cooked low and slow in beef stock with Worcestershire sauce and vegetables, then covered in a thick layer of buttered mash and baked until the top is golden and the filling is bubbling at the edges. It is the most straightforward kind of British cooking: deeply satisfying, completely unfussy, and better the next day.
sourceNotes:
• The term 'cottage pie' appears in print from 1791, when it referred to the practice of using leftover cooked meat under a potato crust — potato being the new affordable crop available to labouring families. The modern version using raw mince cooked in gravy emerged through the nineteenth century.
• The method here draws on the tradition documented in Mrs Beeton, *Book of Household Management* (1861, public domain): the combination of mince, stock, Worcestershire sauce, and a mashed potato crust is the English standard against which all versions are compared.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before or after the final bake. Defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat in a 180°C oven for 25 minutes from cold until piping hot throughout.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles without adjustment. Use a larger baking dish or divide between two.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble up to 2 days ahead, cover, and refrigerate. Bake from cold, adding 10 minutes to the oven time.

glossaryTerms.definition: A British baked dish of minced beef in a savoury gravy, topped with mashed potato and baked until the top is golden. Distinguished from shepherd's pie by the use of beef rather than lamb or mutton.

content.text: requires no technique beyond patience: the mince needs to cook long enough for the stock to reduce into something glossy and savoury, and the potato needs to be well-seasoned and properly mashed before it goes on top. Both are simple. Neither should be hurried.

Use beef mince with at least 20% fat — leaner mince dries out and packs into tight clumps that won't absorb the sauce. The Worcestershire sauce and a small amount of tomato purée are the flavour anchors: don't omit them.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of oil in a large frying pan over a medium-high heat. Brown {{beef-mince}} of mince in batches, breaking it up with a spoon. Don't stir too early — let the meat colour on the base before breaking it apart. Remove the browned mince and set aside.

Reduce the heat to medium. Cook {{onion}} diced onion, {{carrot}} diced carrot, and {{celery}} diced celery in the same pan for 8 minutes until softened. Add {{garlic}} of garlic and {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée. Cook for 2 minutes. Return the mince to the pan. Add {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce, {{stock-beef}} of beef stock, {{thyme-fresh}} of thyme, and {{bay-leaves}}. Bring to a simmer and cook uncovered for 25 minutes until the gravy has reduced and thickened. Remove the herbs and season.

Cook {{potato}} of potato quarters in a large saucepan of salted water for 15 to 18 minutes until completely tender. Drain well. Return to the pan over a low heat for 1 minute to dry out any remaining water. Mash with {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and {{whole-milk}} of warm milk until smooth. Season generously.

Preheat the oven to 200°C (fan 180°C / gas 6). Tip the filling into a large baking dish. Spoon the mash over the top and spread to the edges, making sure to seal the filling in completely. Use a fork to rough up the surface. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the top is golden and the filling is bubbling at the edges. Rest for 5 minutes before serving.

items.symptom: Filling is watery and pools under the mash
items.cause: The gravy was not reduced long enough before assembling.
items.fix: The filling should be thick enough to mound slightly in a spoon before it goes under the mash. Continue reducing until the gravy coats the meat rather than pooling. A watery filling makes the mash sink and turn soggy.

items.symptom: Mash has sunk into the filling during baking
items.cause: Filling too wet, or the mash was too thin.
items.fix: Reduce the filling further and make sure the mash is stiff enough to hold its shape. It should be spreadable but not pourable.

items.symptom: Mince has clumped into large, dense chunks
items.cause: The pan was overcrowded during browning and the meat steamed rather than browned.
items.fix: Brown in batches over high heat. The meat should sizzle loudly on contact with the pan. Once browned, the clumps break up during simmering in the gravy.

items.symptom: Potato topping is gluey and heavy
items.cause: The potatoes were overworked — too much beating or use of a food processor.
items.fix: Mash with a potato masher, not a food processor. Over-worked potato releases starch and becomes gluey. Mash until smooth, no further.

content.text: Cottage pie is named for the cottage-dwellers (rural labourers) who made it from the scraps of the week: leftover cooked meat, vegetables from the garden, and the potato that had become a staple crop by the late eighteenth century. The original was a resourceful dish. The modern version, made with fresh mince and a proper beef stock, is considerably better than the original and has long since escaped its origins to become a standard of British home cooking across every social class. It is one of those dishes that nobody makes to impress anyone, and that is exactly the point.
